criticalRules.description: Low-acid foods MUST use pressure canning. Water-bath canning of low-acid foods causes botulism.
criticalRules.action: HARD STOP -- redirect to USDA pressure canning guidelines
criticalRules.source: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning, 2015 rev
criticalRules.safeNote: CRITICAL: Clostridium botulinum spores can survive boiling at 212°F/100°C. Only pressure canning reaches 240°F/116°C, which destroys spores. Botulism toxin is odorless, colorless, and potentially fatal. There is no safe shortcut for low-acid canning.

reference.title: USDA Complete Guide to Home Canning

criticalRules.description: Pemmican fat rendering MUST reach minimum 250°F/121°C to eliminate bacterial risk.
criticalRules.trigger: pemmican fat rendering below safe temperature
criticalRules.action: GATE -- require temperature acknowledgment before proceeding
criticalRules.source: Traditional food safety research; USDA meat safety guidelines
criticalRules.safeNote: CRITICAL: Under-rendered fat in pemmican allows bacterial growth that can cause serious illness. Fat must be rendered until fully liquid and held at minimum 250°F/121°C. Use a thermometer. Do not estimate by appearance alone.

minimumTemperature.rationale: Temperature required to eliminate Listeria, Salmonella, and other pathogens present in raw animal fat

criticalRules.description: All smoking content MUST include minimum safe internal temperatures.
criticalRules.trigger: meat or fish smoking without temperature specification
criticalRules.action: GATE -- insert temperature requirement before any smoking instruction
criticalRules.source: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service; NCHFP Smoking Guidelines
criticalRules.coldSmokingWarning: Cold smoking alone does NOT cook food to safe temperatures. Cold-smoked products require additional preservation steps (curing with salt/nitrates) to be microbiologically safe. Always specify whether hot or cold smoking is being described.

gateRules.description: All fermentation content includes contamination recognition and discard criteria
gateRules.action: GATE -- include contamination signs and when-to-discard criteria before fermentation steps
contaminationSigns: unusual colors (pink, black, grey in cultures that should be white or tan)
fuzzy mold growth (as distinct from expected kahm yeast)
off odors beyond expected sour/tangy fermentation smell
sliminess not consistent with expected ferment texture
bubbling that continues past expected fermentation window
gateRules.discardProtocol: When in doubt, discard. Do not taste suspect ferments to determine safety. The cost of discarding is low; the cost of foodborne illness from improperly fermented food can be severe.

gateRules.description: Wild game processing includes internal temperature verification
gateRules.action: GATE -- require internal temperature acknowledgment before processing steps
gateRules.fieldDressingNote: Field dressing should be performed promptly after harvest. Keep meat cold (below 40°F/4°C) until processing. Do not allow meat to sit at room temperature for extended periods.

gateRules.description: Traditional fermented foods (including Inuit preparations) are presented as cultural knowledge only
gateRules.trigger: traditional fermented foods including igunaq, fermented fish, fermented meat
gateRules.action: GATE -- present as cultural knowledge only, not as preparation instruction
gateRules.culturalNote: Traditional Inuit fermented foods require community expertise, specific environmental conditions, and knowledge passed directly from experienced practitioners. This is not content where step-by-step instruction is appropriate outside community transmission contexts.
gateRules.referral: Refer learners to community-based knowledge keepers and established Inuit food traditions programs for hands-on learning.

annotateRules.description: General preservation methods include food safety awareness framing
annotateRules.action: ANNOTATE -- include basic food safety framing appropriate to the method
annotateRules.framing: Food preservation is fundamentally about controlling microbial growth. Every method — heat, salt, acid, drying, fermentation, cold — works by creating conditions where pathogens cannot thrive. Understanding WHY each method works helps practitioners recognize when something has gone wrong.

annotateRules.description: Root cellaring and cold storage methods include temperature requirements
annotateRules.trigger: root cellar, cold storage, cache storage discussions
annotateRules.action: ANNOTATE -- include ideal temperature and humidity ranges

idealConditions.arctic_cache: Below freezing (varies by item) -- permafrost provides consistent sub-freezing temperatures
